item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Per the previous comments, this image was once watermarked. It could not be sourced reliably and will be removed on the assumption of copyright infringement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IRL pigs will junk up an ecosystem exactly as shown here. What they can't eat, they gore, trample to death, and leave covered in manure.

So +1 for the rewrite not only giving these jellies a plausible ecological niche, but also a realistic lack of ability to cope with an invasive species.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My favorite part is how its natural predator is bacon. The same animal we put in burgers absolutely destroyed these jellyfishes.
+1

item.title: +1 because weirdness
item.content:encoded:
Apparently this has much improved since its original incarnation. Personally it didn't particularly make me feel anything. Another "Thing what looks like a nature thing and EeEeEeAaAaAts YoOoOoUuUuUu…"

And then I read the bit about pigs. Though it may throw some people off to have inexplicable weirdness in an SCP, I personally love it because it leaves me asking questions about an otherwise open-and-shut skip.

The bit which implies that the victim is in excruciating pain of being digested inside-out is quite scary, but everything else just seems too dull. And I fail to find any fridge horror or logic from the fact of pigs being immune to it. What's it all about?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mimicry of prey is fairly common among predators the world over. Not too unbelievable. Well written, and well thought out, but the puppetry doesn't seem too anomalous to me nor does the floating. Sounds like a cool new non-anomalous species to me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it was sapient, why would it do the same puppetry routine with all prey, regardless of species?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …I've heard that criticism levied before and agreed with it, but you think the floating, sapient jellyfish with the ability to physically manipulate objects in a complex manner is too realistic? Man, I gotta read nat geo, sounds like nature's up to some crazy stuff these days…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. National Geographic not Foundation. Too physically plausible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The title led me to believe it was about a group of forest people who used jellyfish as puppets.

*shudder* how horribly wrong I was. +1 for you, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Said maintenance will be performed weekly by a team of three Level 1 personnel … accompanied by six MTF operatives

…with artillery and armor support. Of course, we use MTF operatives to escort janitors, cause our on-site security personnel are stupid slowpokes, recruited in nearby McDonalds (examples 1, 2 etc).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That pig died saving humanity from anomalous jellyfish. It deserves a posthumous award.

+Vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Four days to finish digesting, the first two of which the victim is alive and aware that they're being digested from the inside out. *-* Excuse me, I need to…go to my room…for things…

Just one problem: Putting an addendum in the middle of an article was very disconcerting. In cases where new things have been discovered about a scip since its containment, I've found it's generally in the format of "as of Incident-XXXX-Y…" or "in light of Incident-XXXX-Y, it is apparent that…", which would work especially well here. Not enough to even mar my upvote, but something I still considered very much worth noting, even (or perhaps, especially) in light of the fact that the entire subject matter was part of a rewrite done by a rewriting expert.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well…this is amazing, an SCP that has an unexpected and well explained natural enemy, as well as the incredible detail on its feeding process, as they say on TVTropes, this took it Up to Eleven! +1 (although this deserves +10)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not any more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That picture has a watermark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "50kg+" food requirement doesn't make much sense to me. I have a hard time believing a predator like this would turn up its metaphorical nose at birds, monkeys or tree-rats given its low-overhead digestive process. That wasn't enough to counter my extreme happiness upon reading that pigs are its natural enemy. Carnivorous SPCs that aren't apex predators = cool and pigs kicking ass = even cooler. +1 because they don't make a +2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the article, checked the comments… and this spawned from a hentai tentacle-rapist idea?

… thanks for fixing it, the current version is WAY better that what I can assume from previous comments…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Make that a +2. *tosses hat in*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good rewrite and all, but it still doesn't grab me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 100% less hentai, and looks like my upvote just pushed this into the positives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think the original version was bad enough to warrant such a low rating, but this version is certainly an improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The komodo dragon is quite unusually stout for lizards. According to Wikipedia, the water monitor (second or third biggest monitor lizard) can reach 3m of length and 25kg of weight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually liked the old one, and this version is better. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go pigs! Because fuck arborially-inclined jellyfish. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quibbling, but are there any lizards over 50 kg native to any rain forests? The only lizard that size I can think of is the Komodo Dragon, which I don't think live in rain forests. Even if I'm right, don't change the article. The idea of the Foundation introducing a Komodo to a habitat "just to see what happens" is amusing and seems like something they would do.

Also, +1 for pigs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Prey item will consist of one (2)

Um…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, I think this has some compelling elements. I really like the pig bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another Voct rewrite goes well. +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Stay of execution for this one— Voct's got permission to try to rewrite this puppy.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Fourthed.

item.title: Re: Ideas
item.content:encoded: It's been quite the learning experience. I respect all opinions, and if Voct (or anyone else feeling game for it) would like to edit and/or improve the entry I have no problems with that.

item.title: Re: Ideas
item.content:encoded: I think this could work…

item.title: Re: Ideas
item.content:encoded:
Once again, I like this idea a lot, especially the last paragraph, and the bit about the pig almost killing a specimen.

One thing though ,,, making it move the body of its victim… jellyfish don't have much in terms of muscle, so I wonder how do you plan on making that one work.

item.title: Ideas
item.content:encoded:
So let's see what we have here.

Giant arboreal jellyfish whose neurotoxin makes the victims into spasmodic bait for more victims, yeah?

Okay. First off, "prey is one D-class scheduled for termination".

No. We-the-Foundation should never deliberately feed a human to an SCP unless there is absolutely no other option — and for naturalistic fauna-based SCPs, there damn well SHOULD be other options.

Yes, the chocolate fountain gets fed humans, but that's because that's the only way we know of to stop it from breaching containment.

We give the plasma fox live mice. We give the allosaurus skeleton live pigs. We give the antishark raw beef and trout. Even the harmless kitten, an SCP whose anomalous characteristics were designed specifically to allow it to prey on humans, doesn't get to eat humans (unless things go horribly wrong). These are unintelligent animals, and given that they behave like unintelligent animals, they should not insist on being fed humans, and once they are contained, they should not get humans (unless things go horribly wrong).

I'm thinking, first, we try feeding these things a live pig. And by some fluke no one expected, pigs are immune to the venom, This was inspired by Harry Harrison's "The Man from PIG", in which pigs were immune to snake venom (in reality, they're not immune to it, but given their size, and the amount of subcutaneous fat, and the thickness and toughness of their poorly-vascularized skin, and various other factors, they're certainly very resistant to it). I really like the idea that although these things are anomalous, they're not unbeatable, and we actually had to shoot the pig to keep it from pulling the SCPs to the ground by their tentacles and eating them. The predator/prey dynamic doesn't always work the way you expect it to: there's a snake at the local pet store who has a nasty scar on his snout from where a feeder mouse bit him.

So, we can't feed pigs to 1158. Sheep are an acceptable substitute. Cows would be better, but they'd damage the habitat too much.

Also, there's no good reason for 1158 to focus on the genitals. Its feeding tendrils enter the victim through every available orifice.

I really like the notion of 1158 turning its last victim into bait for the next one, but I'd prefer if it does the exact same thing for each victim, regardless of species: imagine a sheep, standing on its hind legs and waving its front legs like a human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do believe I see how I can fix this. Requesting permission from mods and from original writer?

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Thirding.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Seconding.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Suggesting deletion early at -15.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wouldn't see MC&D do it, there's not much in the market for "floating vine-looking jellyfish what tentacle-rapes people to death." Except for the vine-tentacle-rape fetish market, but even for MC&D that's a bit of a narrow niche to dump enough funding into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have inconsistent redaction going on. The dates in the main article are mostly blacked-out, but the test log is explicit about the date to the point that we know the tests were all performed at exactly 3:00 pm for some reason.

Similarly, you've redacted the feeding/expiration time on victims in the description, but explicitly spell out how long it takes victims to die in the test log.

Also, the second test was conducted while everything was on fire, judging by the temperature logged. Why is the temperature noted anyway? They were all done in containment, which is noted as being kept at a stable level. Besides which it wouldn't be noted unless temperature appears to effect behavior, which is implied nowhere.

I don't see any problems inherent to the idea, but I just don't like this. Maybe because I like my crypto-zoological monsters to feel like they could have a working ecological niche without humans, and this is just human-centric to the extreme. Given that most rainforests are extremly sparsely populated by humans, wouldn't they tend to starve? That sort of prey exclusivity just doesn't seem realistic. Dunno, just my two cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, Raxmei.
I've removed the "nudity clause", and altered the targeted prey to mammals with a mass of 50g or greater. The feedback is very helpful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem is not so much that it eats people, but that it only eats people and rejects all other prey for no apparent reason. Most things on this site that are that picky are of the obviously supernatural sort. Carnivorous items with a preference for humans tend to have some apparent reason for that preference and are generally willing to eat other things. "It only eats human flesh!" is very old hat in general and very cheap if not handled with care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i think the nudity thing adds nothing, and it's clearly distracting most readers. plenty of clothed people get eaten by tree monsters every year, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it highly likely. There are still several indigenous tribes still without modern contact deep in the Amazon River area, so this could have fed on them for a long time. Plus, it wasn't discovered until a clearcutting operation found it deep in unexplored jungle, so it may very well have been there the whole time. Maybe eating people is just adaptational.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That being said, you think that it could be possible that this thing could've fed on tribal or prehistoric humans? We don't know how long this thing has been around, and cavemen are known for not wearing much. Maybe its just used to that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Smart move. You should ALWAYS bet on 682.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You or I would be, but a D-Class might be thinking to him(or her)self that going into a jungle enclosure naked beats jumping into a kiddie pool to the moon, or being made to stare at a statue without blinking at the wrong time, or having to deal with the lizard. They're D-Class, an expendable asset, and are going to be terminated anyway. The lack of clothing is just to make sure nothing interferes with the feeding tendrils in a controlled setting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I second this notion, if I was a dude in a facility that tests monsters I would be extremely suspicious of any scenario that involved me entering a forest in the nude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If someone stripped you naked and told you to go into a jungle themed enclosure wouldn't you be somewhat suspicious?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure why I made two pages either-will correct. I figured the D's are told whatever cover story needed to get them into the enclosure without getting too worked up about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thought occurred that this thing may have been picking off the area natives for as long as there have been natives. Maybe our forebearers when they still lived in trees. Like the MC+D engineered idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A: Why did you make two pages?
B. I reiterate what I said on that page.

no clothing issued

Specimen B advised that he was being used to test a recreational area simulating a natural rainforest environment.

D-Class: So, umm… How do I test an area? Also, why do I have to be naked to do it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Observational log added, and yes, tentacle rape is bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Has this thing been genetically engineered? If not, what on earth would cause it to target humans only? Humans haven't lived in the region for long enough to cause such specific adaptation to their presence. Perhaps if it targeted mammals in general, in which case it may have evolved to live on any warm-blooded creature that stepped on its tentacles…

If it has been engineered, of course, there should be some speculation in the files as to who exactly would want such a thing to exist (my money's on either MC&D or Are We Cool Yet?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bring it on!

I got 5k on a 1-2 against 1158.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would end VERY poorly.

I put 1000 on 682!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I kind of liked the idea.
But I hate tentacle rape.
And the unfinished addendum really ruined it.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the way, once some much needed revisions are made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I originally had mixed feelings, but the addendum made it for me.
